S29: Søk etter studier ble avslut- tet november 2015 6 Key messages Key messages The Norwegian Knowledge Centre for the Health Services was commissioned by the Norwegian Directorate of Health to con‐ duct a systematic literature search of systematic reviews and on‐ going primary studies on the treatment and follow‐up of patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
S30: Methods We conducted a systematic literature search in several data‐ bases.
S31: The search was completed in November 2015, and rele‐ vant references were sorted according to subject.
S32: Results We identified 132 potential systematic reviews and 141 ongoing primary studies:  Most of the systematic reviews were about the effect of different drug treatments.
S33: There were also many potential systematic reviews on ventilation support, physical therapy and exercise.
S34:  12 systematic reviews summarized qualitative research on the perceptions and experiences of patients and their carers.
S35:  3 systematic reviews were about the correlation betweeen different factors and the patients’ quality of life.
S36:  Most of the ongoing primary studies were about the effect of different drug treatments.
S37: There were also a number of studies on stem cell treatment and ventilation support.
S38: Five of the studies were from a Nordic country.
S39: We have not critically evaluated the studies and not read them in full text.
S40: Therefore, we cannot draw any conclusions regarding the studies' results, and some of the identified overviews may not be truly systematic reviews.
S41: We did not search for completed and published primary studies.
S42: We present abstracts from the identified systematic reviews and URLs to the ongoing primary studies.

S166: Bucchia M, Ramirez A, Parente V, Simone C, Niz‐ zardo M, Magri F, et al.
S167: Ther‐ apeutic development in amyotrophic lateral scle‐ rosis.
S169: PURPOSE: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is the most common motor neuron disease in adults.
S170: It is almost invariably lethal within a few years after the onset of symptoms.
S171: No effective treatment is currently available beyond supportive care and riluzole, a putative glutamate release blocker linked to modestly prolonged survival.
S172: This review provides a general overview of preclinical and clinical advances during recent years and summarizes the literature regarding emerging therapeutic approaches, focusing on their molecular targets.
S173: METHODS: A systematic literature review of PubMed was performed, identifying key clinical trials involving molecular therapies for ALS.
S174: In addition, the ALS Therapy Development Institute website was carefully analyzed, and a selection of ALS clinical trials registered at ClinicalTrials.gov has been included.
S175: FINDINGS: In the last several years, strategies have been developed to understand both the genetic and molecular mechanisms of ALS.
S176: Several therapeutic targets have been actively pursued, including kinases, inflammation inhibitors, silencing of key genes, and modulation or replacement of specific cell populations.
S177: The majority of ongoing clinical trials are investigating the safety profiles and tolerability of pharmacologic, gene, and cellular therapies, and have begun to assess their effects on ALS progression.
S178: IMPLICATIONS: Currently, no therapeutic effort seems to be efficient, but recent findings in ALS could help accelerate the discov‐ ery of an effective treatment for this disease.Copyright © 2015 Elsevier HS Journals, Inc. All rights reserved.
S180: Gould RL, Coulson MC, Brown RG, Goldstein LH, Al‐ Our objective was to systematically review and critically evaluate the evidence for psychotherapy and pharmacotherapy interven‐ tions for reducing distress or improving well‐being in people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (pwALS).
S181: Online bibliographic 16 Resultater Chalabi A, Howard RJ.
S182: Psy‐ chotherapy and pharma‐ cotherapy interventions to reduce distress or improve well‐being in people with amyotrophic lateral scle‐ rosis: A systematic review.
S183: Amyotrophic Lateral Sclero‐ sis and Frontotemporal De‐ generation 2015;16(5‐ 6):293‐302.
S184: databases and clinical trial registers were searched and an assessment of study quality was conducted.
S185: Seven thousand two hun‐ dred and twenty‐three studies were identified, of which five met inclusion criteria (four completed and one in progress).
S186: All stud‐ ies examined psychotherapeutic interventions, and no studies investigated pharmacotherapy.
S187: Two studies adopted a randomized controlled trial design, one a controlled trial design and two a cohort design.
S188: Sample sizes were small in all studies (overall n = 145).
S189: The quality of completed studies was generally poor, with evidence that all were at potential risk of bias in numerous areas.
S190: Improvements in well‐being were found with expressive disclosure (compared to no disclosure), cognitive behavioural ther‐ apy/counselling (compared to non‐randomized pharmacotherapy) and hypnosis in the short term only, while no improvements were seen with a life review intervention.
S191: In conclusion, there is currently insufficient evidence to recommend the use of specific psychotherapy interventions for reducing distress or improving well‐being in pwALS, and no evidence to support pharmacother‐ apy interventions.
S192: Research is urgently needed to address these significant gaps in the literature.
S194: Oliver D, Borasio GD, Voltz R, Caraceni A, De Visser M, Lo‐ renzl S, et al.
S195: The develop‐ ment of a consensus paper on palliative care in neu‐ rology‐the implications for ALS care.
S196: Amyotrophic Lat‐ eral Sclerosis and Fronto‐ temporal Degeneration 2014;15:117‐118.
S197: Background: A joint working group of the European Association for Palliative Care and the European Federation of Neurological Societies has produced a Consensus paper on the available evidence for the palliative care in progressive neurological disease.
S198: This has been a generic paper looking at all progressive neurological disease ‐ including ALS/MND, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson ' s disease, stroke and primary brain tumours.
S199: The principles need to be considered separately for all disease groups as well ‐ includ‐ ing ALS/ MND.
S200: Objectives: To show the important areas for consideration in the palliative care for people with ALS/MND.
S201: Meth‐ ods: A literature search was undertaken including the main electronic databases and looking at the main areas of palliative care and neurology.
S202: Two investigators then looked at this literature, determined seven main areas and developed a draft list of papers.
S203: These were then commented on by a small group and then more widely until a consensus was developed.
S204: Results: The seven main areas all apply to the care of people with ALS/MND, and it could be argued that with the short prognosis of ALS/MND and the vari‐ able rate of progression this approach should be from the time of diagnosis.
S205: The areas of recommendation are: Palliative care should be considered early in the disease trajectory; the assessment and care should be provided by a multidisciplinary team ap‐ proach, with access to specialist palliative care; communication should be open with patients and families and advance care plan‐ ning is recommended.
S206: This should be as soon as possible in view of the likelihood of difficulties in communication and the devel‐ opment of cognitive change in ALS/MND; symptoms ‐ physical and psychosocial ‐ should be managed actively and appropriately; care needs should be assessed and carers supported before and after death.
S207: Professional carers should receive education, support and supervision to reduce the risks of emotional exhaustion; there should be repeated and continued discussion about end of life issues and discussion of patients ' wishes and aims.
S208: The recognition of the deterioration and dying phase will allow appropriate management and intervention; palliative care principles should be included with the training and continuing medical education of neurologists and palliative care professionals should understand the issues for neurological patients.
S209: Discussion and conclusion: The recommendations within the Consensus document can be seen to apply to the care of people with ALS/MND and there is a challenge to ensure that these principles are extended as widely as possible to support people with ALS/MND and their carers.

S214: OBJECTIVE: To review evidences on the management of patients with motor neuron disease.

S216: STUDY SELECTION: Key words for literature search were "motor neuron disease review (MND)".
S217: Only the articles which concen‐ trated on the ventilation, nutrition, cognitive or multidisciplinary approaches for motor neuron disease were included.
S218: Case re‐ ports were not included in the review.
S219: In addition, publications were identified using the World Wide Web from references in these papers.
S220: Only articles in English were considered.
S221: DATA EXTRACTION: A total of 782 articles were retrieved using the key word search, of which 72 concentrated on ventilation, nutrition, cognitive or multidisciplinary approaches.
S222: From these, 43 articles were eventually included and formed the basis of this review.
S223: DATA SYNTHESIS: Motor neuron disease is an adult‐onset neurodegenerative disease that leads to weakness of limb, bulbar, and respiratory muscles.
S224: It displays an ethnic variation in incidence; 90% of cases are sporadic and 10% are familial.
S225: New diagnostic criteria have been proposed to increase diagnostic sensitivity.
S226: Proper clinical studies, electrophysiology, and neuroimaging are necessary before reaching a diagnosis of motor neuron disease.
S227: Riluzole remains the only disease‐modifying drug approved for this disease; it prolongs life by 3 to 4 months.
S228: Multidisciplinary care units are important in the management of motor neuron dis‐ ease patients.
S229: Non‐invasive positive pressure ventilation prolongs life in motor neuron disease patients with respiratory failure.
S230: Enteral feeding is usually recommended for affected patients with malnutrition.
S231: Cognitive impairment is common in these pa‐ tients, for whom a formal neuropsychiatric assessment is recommended.
S232: Appropriate palliative care is needed for these patients in order to improve their quality of dying.
S233: CONCLUSION: Motor neuron disease is an incurable disease, for which a highly effective treatment is still pending.
S234: Symptomatic treatment remains the mainstay of management.
S235: A multidisciplinary approach embracing advances in non‐invasive ventilation and gastrostomy can improve quality of life and extend the survival of motor neuron disease patients.

S316: Background: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is a rare neurodegenerative dis‐ ease.
S317: Approximately 5% to 7% of ALS/MND patients report a family history of a similarly affected relative.
S318: Superoxide dismutase‐ 1 gene mutations are the cause in about 20% of familial cases.
S319: In those with non‐familial (sporadic) ALS/MND the cause is un‐ known.
S320: Also unknown is whether patients with familial and sporadic ALS/MND respond differently to treatment.
S321: Objectives: To systematically review the literature and to answer the specific question: 'Is there a difference in the response to treatment be‐ tween patients with sporadic and familial forms of ALS?'
S322: Search strategy: In May 2006 we searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Trials Register, MEDLINE (January 1966 to May 2006) and EMBASE (January 1980 to May 2006) for randomized 20 Resultater controlled trials (RCTs).
S323: Two review authors read the titles and abstracts of all articles and reviewed the full text of all possibly relevant articles.
S324: We scanned references of all included trials to identify additional relevant articles.
S325: For all trials eligible for inclu‐ sion we contacted the authors to request the necessary raw data.
S326: Selection criteria: Studies had to meet two criteria: (a) random‐ ized controlled study design, and (b) inclusion of patients with both familial and sporadic ALS/MND.
S327: Data collection and analysis: We attempted to contact authors of all trials that met inclusion criteria.
S328: We obtained data regarding ALS/MND type (sporadic ver‐ sus familial), treatment assignment (active versus placebo), survival and ALS Functional Rating Scale scores for four large RCTs that included 822 sporadic and 41 familial ALS patients.
S329: We could not obtain data from 25 potentially eligible studies (17 trial authors could not be contacted and eight were unwilling to provide data).
S330: There was no statistical evidence for a different re‐ sponse to treatment in patients with familial ALS/MND compared to those with sporadic ALS/MND.
S331: The pooled estimate of the hazard ratio for the interaction term (treatment x familial ALS) suggested a more beneficial response with respect to survival among patients with familial ALS/MND, but the result was not statistically significant.
S332: Estimates of the rate of decline on the ALS Functional Rating Scale also suggested a slightly better response to treatment among those with familial ALS/MND, but the result was not statistically significant.
S333: Authors' conclusions: Future RCTs should document whether patients with familial ALS/MND are included and the presence or absence of a mutation in the superoxide dismutase‐1 gene amongst those with familial ALS/MND.

S366: Background: The American Academy of Neurology (AAN) issued an evidence‐based report on managing patients with amyo‐ trophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) in 1999.
S367: Objective: To systematically review evidence bearing on the management of patients with ALS and update the 1999 AAN practice parameter.
S368: Methods: The authors completed a systematic literature review from 1998 to 2008.
S369: Topics included breaking the news, symptom management, slowing disease progression, nutrition, respiratory manage‐ ment, palliative care, cognitive and behavioral impairment, multidisciplinary clinics, and communication for patients with ALS.
S370: Results: The authors identified 10 Class I studies, 13 Class II studies, and 73 Class III studies in ALS.
S371: More studies are clearly needed to examine the best tests of respiratory function in ALS, the optimal time for starting PEG, the impact of PEG on quality of life and survival, the effect of vitamins and supplements, symptomatic therapies and palliative care.
S372: The following recommenda‐ tions are made based on the studies analyzed: Riluzole should be offered to slow disease progression (Level A).
S373: Percutaneous en‐ doscopic gastrostomy (PEG) should be considered to stabilize weight and to prolong survival (Level B).
S374: Noninvasive ventilation (NIV) should be considered to treat respiratory insufficiency in order to lengthen survival (Level B), and may be considered to slow the decline of forced vital capacity (Level C) and improve quality of life (Level C).
S375: Early initiation of NIV may increase compli‐ ance (Level C), and insufflation/exsufflation may be considered to help clear secretions (Level C).
S376: Multidisciplinary clinic referral should be considered to optimize health care delivery and prolong survival (Level B) and may be considered to enhance quality of life (Level C).
S377: For the treatment of refractory sialorrhea, botulinum toxin B should be considered (Level B) and low‐dose radiation therapy to the salivary glands may be considered (Level C).
S378: For treatment of pseudobulbar affect, the combination therapy of dex‐ tro‐methorphan with quinidine should be considered, though side effects are not uncommon and the treatment is currently not approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (Level B).
S379: For patients who develop fatigue while taking riluzole, withholding the drug may be considered (Level C).
S380: Because many patients with ALS demonstrate cognitive impairment, which in some cases meets criteria for dementia, screening for cognitive and behavioral impairment should be considered in patients with ALS (Level B).
S381: Other management strategies all lack strong evidence.
S382: Discussion: There are many treatments available for patients with ALS that can alleviate suffering.
S383: NIV, PEG, riluzole, and multidisciplinary clinics are the most important and have the best evidence.
S384: Conclusions: More high‐quality, controlled studies are needed to guide management and to assess outcomes in patients with ALS.

S558: We found no statistically significant difference in cases where the technique was not reported (RR 0.43, 95% CI 0.13 to 1.44, four studies, 285 participants).There was no statistically significant difference between the groups for meta‐ analyses of the secondary outcomes of mortality (RR 0.86, 95% CI 0.58 to 1.28, 644 participants, nine studies, very low quality evidence), overall reports of any adverse event at any follow‐up time point (ITT analysis, RR 0.83, 95% CI 0.51 to 1.34), 597 participants, 6 studies, moderate quality evidence), specific adverse events in‐ cluding pneumonia (aspiration) (RR 0.70, 95% CI 0.46 to 1.06, 645 participants, seven studies, low quality evi‐ dence), or for the meta‐ analyses of the secondary outcome of nutritional status including weight change from baseline, and mid‐arm circumference at endpoint, although there was evidence in favour of PEG for meta‐anal‐ yses of mid‐arm circumference change from baseline (MD 1.16, 95% CI 1.01 to 1.31, 115 participants, two stud‐ ies), and levels of serum albumin were higher in the PEG group (MD 6.03, 95% CI 2.31 to 9.74, 107 partici‐ pants).For meta‐analyses of the secondary outcomes of time on enteral nutrition, there was no statistically sig‐ nificant difference (MD 14.48, 95% CI ‐2.74 to 31.71; 119 participants, two studies).

S1145: Der BACKGROUND: Various recommendations exist for the treatment of nausea and vomiting in palliative care but only few studies and even less systematic reviews look into antiemetic therapy for patients re‐ ceiving palliative care.
S1146: OBJECTIVES: This systematic review aims to analyze the current evidence for antiemetic treatment with 5HT3 receptor antagonists, steroids, antihistamines, anticholinergics, somatostatin analogs, benzodiaze‐ pines and cannabinoids in palliative care patients with far advanced cancer not receiving chemotherapy or radiotherapy, acquired immune deficiency syndrome (AIDS), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), progressive heart failure, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) or multiple sclerosis (MS).

S1152: RESULTS: In the general search 75 relevant studies were found.
S1153: Of those 36 addressed 5HT3 receptor antagonists, steroids, antihistamines, anticholinergics, somatostatin analogs, benzodiazepines and can‐ nabinoids, 13 considered 5HT3 receptor antagonists, 10 somatostatin antagonists, 9 steroids, 5 canna‐ binoids, 4 anticholinergics, 1 antihistamines and none benzodiazepines.
S1154: Furthermore six systematic re‐ views exist.
S1155: Evidence for any drug used as an antiemetic is low.
S1156: Concerning 5HT3 receptor antagonists data are insufficient for recommendations on the treatment of patients with AIDS and MS due to the small size of included patient groups.
S1157: For patients with cancer contradictory results were published: the larger studies showed a positive effect of 5HT3 receptor antagonists and better efficacy, as compared to metoclopramide, dexamethasone and neuroleptics.

S1162: Canna‐ binoids were found to relieve nausea and vomiting in patients with cancer and AIDS but with notable side effects.

S1164: Regarding symptom control of nausea and vomiting in patients with COPD, progressive heart failure and ALS no studies were undertaken in patients receiving palliative care.

S1167: Canna‐ binoids rather have a status as a second line antiemetic.

S1169: Concerning antihistamines and benzodiazepines insufficient data was found.
S1170: Recom‐ mendations in the literature are mainly based on studies in patients with cancer.
S1171: The overall strength of evidence is low.
S1172: More well designed studies in palliative care patients are needed in order to provide evi‐ dence‐based therapy.

METHODS: Two different electronic databases (PubMed und Embase) were used to identify studies.
S1181: Fur‐ thermore, a hand search for related articles was performed.
S1182: No restriction was made concerning study types.

S1185: All studies focused on cancer patients.
S1186: Despite intensive research studies in patients with AIDS, COPD, heart failure, ALS or MS were not detected.

S1191: The evidence level for prokinetics and neuroleptics is moderate to low.
S1192: Concerning palliative care of patients with dis‐ eases other than cancer no studies exist.
S1193: More well designed studies in palliative care patients are needed in order to facilitate evidence based antiemetic therapy.

S1723: BACKGROUND: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is a progressive neurodegenerative disease without effective therapies.
S1724: Several studies have suggested that repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) may have positive benefit in ALS.
S1725: However, the efficacy and safety of this therapy remain uncertain.
S1726: This is the first update of a review published in 2011.
S1727: OBJECTIVES: To determine the clinical efficacy and safety of rTMS for treating ALS.
S1728: SEARCH METHODS: On 30 July 2012, we searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Regis‐ ter, CENTRAL (2012, issue 7 in The Cochrane Library), MEDLINE (1966 to July 2012), EMBASE (1980 to July 2012), CINAHL (1937 to July 2012), Science Citation Index Expanded (January 1945 to July 2012), AMED (January 1985 to July 2012).
S1729: We searched the Chinese Biomedical Database (1979 to August 2012).
S1730: We also searched for ongoing studies on clinicaltrials.gov (August 2012).
S1731: SELECTION CRITERIA: Randomised and quasi‐randomised controlled trials assessing the therapeutic efficacy and safety of rTMS for patients with a clinical diagnosis of ALS.Comparisons eligible for inclusion were:1. rTMS versus no intervention;2. rTMS versus sham rTMS;3. rTMS versus physiotherapy;4. rTMS versus medications;5. rTMS + other therapies or drugs versus sham rTMS + the same therapies or drugs;6. different methods of application of rTMS such as high‐frequency (> 1Hz) compared to low‐frequency (< 1Hz) rTMS.
S1732: DATA COLLECTION AND ANALYSIS: Two authors independently selected papers, assessed risk of bias and ex‐ tracted data.
S1734: We contacted study authors for additional infor‐ mation.
S1735: MAIN RESULTS: Three randomised, placebo‐controlled trials with a total of 50 participants were included in the review.
S1736: All three trials compared rTMS with sham TMS.
S1737: All the trials were of poor methodological quality and were insufficiently homogeneous to allow the pooling of results.
S1738: Moreover, the high rate of attrition further in‐ creased the risk of bias.
S1739: None of the trials provided detailed data on the ALS Functional Rating Scale‐Revised 74 Resultater (ALSFRS‐R) scores at six months follow‐up which was pre‐assigned as our primary outcome.
S1740: One trial contained data in a suitable form for quantitative analysis of our secondary outcomes.
S1741: No difference was seen between rTMS and sham rTMS using the ALSFRS‐R scores and manual muscle testing (MMT) scores at 12 months follow‐up in this trial.
S1742: Additionally, none of the trials reported any adverse events associated with the use of rTMS.
S1743: However, in view of the small sample size, the methodological limitations and incomplete outcome data, treatment with rTMS cannot be judged as completely safe.
S1744: AUTHORS' CONCLUSIONS: There is currently insufficient evidence to draw conclusions about the efficacy and safety of rTMS in the treatment of ALS.
S1745: Further studies may be helpful if their potential benefit is weighed against the impact of participation in a randomised controlled trial on people with ALS.

S1800: BACKGROUND: Chronic alveolar hypoventilation is a common complication of many neuromuscular and chest wall disorders.
S1801: Long‐term nocturnal mechanical ventilation is commonly used to treat it.
S1802: This is a 2014 update of a review first published in 2000 and previously updated in 2007.
S1803: OBJECTIVES: To examine the effects on mortality of nocturnal mechanical ventilation in people with neuro‐ muscular or chest wall disorders.
S1804: Subsidiary endpoints were to examine the effects of respiratory assistance on improvement of chronic hypoventilation, sleep quality, hospital admissions and quality of life.
S1805: SEARCH METHODS: We searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group Specialized Register, CENTRAL, MEDLINE and EMBASE on 10 June 2014.
S1806: We contacted authors of identified trials and other experts in the field.
S1807: SELECTION CRITERIA: We searched for quasi‐randomised or randomised controlled trials of participants of all ages with neuromuscular or chest wall disorder‐related stable chronic hypoventilation of all degrees of se‐ verity, receiving any type and any mode of long‐term nocturnal mechanical ventilation.
S1808: The primary outcome measure was one‐year mortality and secondary outcomes were unplanned hospital admission, short‐term and long‐term reversal of hypoventilation‐related clinical symptoms and daytime hypercapnia, improvement of lung function and sleep breathing disorders.
S1809: DATA COLLECTION AND ANALYSIS: We used standard Cochrane methodology to select studies, extract data and assess the risk of bias in included studies.
S1810: MAIN RESULTS: The 10 eligible trials included a total of 173 participants.
S1811: Roughly half of the trials were at low risk of selection, attrition or reporting bias, and almost all were at high risk of performance and detection bias.
S1812: Four trials reported mortality data in the long term.
S1813: The pooled risk ratio (RR) of dying was 0.62 (95% confi‐ dence interval (CI) 0.42 to 0.91, P value = 0.01) in favour of nocturnal mechanical ventilation compared to spontaneous breathing.
S1814: There was considerable and significant heterogeneity between the trials, possibly re‐ lated to differences between the study populations.
S1815: Information on unplanned hospitalisation was available from two studies.
S1816: The corresponding pooled RR was 0.25 (95% CI 0.08 to 0.82, P value = 0.02) in favour of nocturnal mechanical ventilation.
S1817: For most of the outcome measures there was no significant long‐term differ‐ ence between nocturnal mechanical ventilation and no ventilation.
S1818: Most of the secondary outcomes were not assessed in the eligible trials.
S1819: Three out of the 10 trials, accounting for 39 participants, two with a cross‐over design and one with two parallel groups, compared volume‐ and pressure‐cycled non‐invasive mechanical ventilation in the short term.
S1820: From the only trial (16 participants) on parallel groups, there was no difference in mortality (one death in each arm) between volume‐ and pressure‐cycled mechanical ventilation.
S1821: Data from the two cross‐over trials suggested that compared with pressure‐cycled ventilation, volume‐cycled ventilation was associated with less sleep time spent with an arterial oxygen saturation below 90% (mean difference (MD) 6.83 minutes, 95% CI 4.68 to 8.98, P value = 0.00001) and a lower apnoea‐hypopnoea (per sleep hour) 79 Resultater index (MD ‐0.65, 95% CI ‐0.84 to ‐0.46, P value = 0.00001).
S1822: We found no study that compared invasive and non‐invasive mechanical ventilation or intermittent positive pressure versus negative pressure ventilation.
S1823: AUTHORS' CONCLUSIONS: Current evidence about the therapeutic benefit of mechanical ventilation is of very low quality, but is consistent, suggesting alleviation of the symptoms of chronic hypoventilation in the short term.

S2144: The symptom list was derived from the American Academy of Neurology ALS Practice Parameters (1).
S2145: Studies were excluded if their aim was to evaluate the effect of a therapy on QoL or if they provided no data on correlation between symptom and QoL.

S2166: Methods: Literature searches were conducted in Medline, Psychinfo, Cochrane Library, and the Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis and Frontotemporal Dementia journal to identify studies reporting relationships between psychosocial factors and QOL in people with MND/ALS.
S2167: Results: One hundred and three potential studies were retrieved after assessing the abstracts of publications identified through the searches.
S2168: Of these, 75 were omitted on the basis of exclusion criteria such as reviews, case studies, diseases other than MND/ALS, outcomes other than QOL, psychosocial factors not included, and not published in English.
S2169: This left 28 studies meeting the eligibility criteria.
S2170: Five psychosocial factors were identified: These were social support; affective state (depression, anxiety, and hopelessness); religion/spirituality; coping strategies; and personality traits.
S2171: Fifteen studies re‐ ported a positive relationship between social support and QOL.
S2172: There were discrepancies among studies investigating affective state.
S2173: Eight studies found that depression was negatively associated with QOL, whereas six found no significant relationship.
S2174: Likewise, findings on anxiety were inconsistent.
S2175: Four studies reported a negative association between anxiety and QOL while three found no relationship.
S2176: Only two studies investigated hopelessness and in both it was negatively associated with QOL.
S2177: While religion/ spirituality was a positive factor in eight studies, one study disputed this.
S2178: Coping strategies (two studies) and personal‐ ity traits (two studies) received limited attention.
S2179: Discussion and conclusion: Social support has the strongest and most con‐ sistent evidence as a factor associated with QOL in MND/ALS.
S2180: Discrepancies in the literature on affective state may be attributa‐ ble to methodological considerations, such as differences in how QOL is conceptualised or measured.
S2181: Fewer studies investigated anxiety than depression, despite several studies finding anxiety to be more strongly associated than depression with QOL.
S2182: Anxi‐ ety may therefore represent an underestimated problem.
S2183: Future research should establish its role.
S2184: While the potential influence 94 Resultater of religion/ spirituality on QOL is strongly supported in the literature, one study found that patients infrequently identified it as a relevant factor.
S2185: That study was conducted in a secularised country (Sweden).
S2186: Future research should establish whether reli‐ gion/spirituality is an important factor across different populations of MND/ALS patients.
S2187: Coping strategies and personality traits warrant additional investigation.
S2188: Of further note, all reviewed studies were observational.
S2189: Interventional designs are re‐ quired to investigate whether interventions targeting psychosocial factors positively affect QOL.

S2219: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a devastating neurological disease for which there is no cure, and the associated economic burden is considerable.

S2230: Objectives: Neurodegenerative diseases (NDs) refer to a group of diseases that affect brain cells.
S2231: Alzheimer disease (AD), Parkin‐ son disease (PD), Amyotrophique Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) and Multiple Sclerosis (MS) are the most prevalent NDs.
S2232: NDs cause a substantial economic burden worldwide and indirect costs are an important component of total costs.
S2233: This study aims to review relevant papers to characterize the different components of indirect costs and to identify the weight of indirect costs on total costs in different NDs.
S2234: Methods: A systematic bibliographic search was performed on an international medical literature database (MEDLINE).
S2235: All studies which assessed the social economic burden and indirect costs of different NDs were selected.
S2236: Indirect costs were characterized into several types (i.e.
S2237: sick leave, presenteeism, early retirement, premature death, reduction in work‐ ing hours, informal care time) and into several valuation (i.e.
S2238: Human Capital Approach, Friction Cost Method, Willingness To Pay).
S2240: Depending on studies, the percentage of indirect costs on total costs varies from 1% to 68% in PD, from 2% to 89% in AD, from 24% to 59% in ALS and from 29% to 78% in MS.
S2241: The main indirect costs component was early retirement in PD, ALS and MS.
S2242: This component varies from 61% to 95% of indirect costs in PD, from 35% to 88% in ALS and from 61% to 95% in MS.
S2243: The main indirect costs component in AD was informal care time and account for almost 100% of indirect costs.
S2244: Indirect costs increase with severity level in AD and MS, and decrease with severity level in ALS.
S2245: Conclusions: Com‐ ponents of indirect costs are different depending on studies and especially for AD where indirect costs mainly refer to informal costs which should be considered as a full cost category to avoid the lack of understanding.

S2346: Incidence and prevalence of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis in Canada: a systematic review of the BACKGROUND: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a fatal progressive neurodegenerative disease of unknown etiology.
S2347: Alt‐ hough known to be rare, precise information on the frequency of ALS is essential to anticipate future demands on health re‐ sources and as baseline information for epidemiological studies.
S2348: As part of a new ALS epidemiological initiative in Canada, we conducted a systematic review of published incidence and prevalence research in Canada.
S2349: METHODS: Electronic searches and bibliographic reviews of pertinent publications were conducted.
S2352: RESULTS: We identified 6 published studies from 4 Canadian provinces conducted between 1974 and 2004; 2 were available only as abstracts.
S2353: Reported annual incidence rates were similar and study quality was generally good, but there was insufficient detail to adequately assess the methodological quality of 3 of the studies.
S2354: The most recent studies reported an annual ALS age‐ adjusted incidence of 2.13 per 100,000 in Nova Scotia (2003‐2004) and a crude mean annual incidence of 2.4 per 100,000 in Newfoundland and Labrador (2000‐2004).
S2355: CONCLUSIONS: There are limited data on the frequency of ALS in Canada.
S2356: We found no studies from 6 of the Canadian provinces or from the territories.
S2357: Future research is needed to estimate the frequency of occurrence of ALS in Canada.Copyright 2009 S. Karger AG, Basel.
